insights: Expenses under $2,000 take 4.9 times longer to process on average (14.1 days) than expenses over $8,000 (2.9 days), revealing a strong inverse relationship between cost and processing time that directly contradicts typical efficiency assumptions.

All 7 departments and 5 locations exhibit nearly identical negative correlations between expense amount and processing time (Pearson r = −0.67 to −0.88, all p < 0.001), confirming the disparity is systemic—not driven by departmental, locational, or group-level bias.

The $2,000 threshold marks the steepest inflection point in processing time, with a 9.17-day gap between adjacent bins—making it the most actionable intervention boundary for fast-tracking low-cost expenses without compromising equity.

94.7% of expenses under $2,000 require more than 7 days to process, and 73.7% exceed 10 days, indicating chronic delay for low-cost items and a high-risk operational bottleneck in routine, lower-value reporting.

No evidence of inequity exists across any organizational dimension: processing time patterns are structurally consistent across departments, locations, and users when controlling for expense amount bins, ruling out discrimination or inconsistent governance as explanatory factors.

The strongest predictive signal for fast processing is high expense amount—not agent assignment, location, or department—suggesting current workflows implicitly prioritize high-dollar items, creating structural inefficiency for the majority of submissions (which are low-cost).
summary: The analysis reveals a counterintuitive but robust inverse relationship between expense amount and processing time: low-cost expenses (<$2,000) take nearly five times longer to process than high-cost ones (> $8,000), with 95% exceeding 7 days. This pattern is uniformly observed across all 7 departments and 5 locations—with no statistical evidence of differential treatment—confirming it is embedded in the end-to-end workflow, not attributable to group-level bias or inconsistency. The $2,000 threshold represents the clearest operational inflection point, separating chronically delayed routine expenses from consistently rapid high-value ones. Given that low-cost expenses constitute the vast majority of submissions, this systemic delay creates both efficiency drag and perceived inequity among employees submitting standard reports. The immediate focus should be implementing a fast-track SLA (≤5 days) for expenses ≤$2,000, monitored via bin-stratified processing time metrics to ensure equitable execution across all teams.
